name: Giant Skeletons Full
description: The 'Giant Skeletons' theory, prevalent in American alternative history circles, posits that the North American continent was once inhabited by a race of giants, evidenced by alleged discoveries of oversized human skeletons. Proponents often cite newspaper articles from the 19th and early 20th centuries reporting on such finds, along with Native American legends referencing giants. They suggest these giants were either wiped out by later waves of human migration or were the ancestors of certain Native American tribes. Some connect these alleged giants to the Nephilim mentioned in the Bible or to a lost, advanced civilization. However, mainstream archaeology and anthropology largely dismiss these claims. The cited newspaper articles are often attributed to sensationalism, hoaxes, or misidentification of fossilized remains of large animals. Skeptics point out the lack of verifiable scientific evidence, such as peer-reviewed studies or well-documented excavations, to support the existence of giant human skeletons in North America. Furthermore, they argue that the selective interpretation of Native American legends ignores the diversity and complexity of indigenous cultures and their oral traditions. The theory persists due to its appeal to those seeking alternative narratives of American history and its connection to broader themes of lost civilizations and suppressed knowledge. The lack of scientifically rigorous documentation has resulted in the theory being relegated to pseudoscience.
subtitle: Claims of ancient giant humanoids in America

claims.claim: Giant skeletons have been found across North America.
claims.evidence: 19th and early 20th century newspaper articles reporting discoveries of giant skeletons.
claims.criticism: Newspaper articles are often sensationalized, lack scientific rigor, and may describe misidentified animal remains. No verified scientific evidence exists.

claims.claim: Native American legends corroborate the existence of giants.
claims.evidence: Certain Native American stories mention beings of great size and strength.
claims.criticism: Legends are diverse and open to interpretation. Selective quoting ignores the complexity of Native American cultures. Many tribes do not have legends of giants.

claims.claim: The Smithsonian Institution has suppressed evidence of giant skeletons.
claims.evidence: Anecdotal accounts and conspiracy theories claim the Smithsonian has confiscated and hidden giant skeletons to protect the prevailing scientific narrative.
claims.criticism: No credible evidence supports this claim. The Smithsonian's collections are well-documented and accessible to researchers.

keyFigures.name: Vine Deloria Jr.
keyFigures.role: Native American author who explored the giant skeleton claims in his writings

keyFigures.name: David Hatcher Childress
keyFigures.role: Author and proponent of alternative archaeology who promotes the giant skeleton theory

keyFigures.name: Jason Colavito
keyFigures.role: Skeptic and researcher who debunks claims of giant skeletons and ancient mysteries

mythologyConnections: Various Native American creation myths and legends

timeline.event: Peak of reported giant skeleton discoveries in newspapers
Resurgence of interest in giant skeleton theories through alternative history publications
Continued promotion of the theory online and in alternative media
